data.title: Citi downgrades Airbus to 'neutral' on impact of weak USD
data.description:
** Citi downgrades European aircraft manufacturer Airbus to "neutral" from "buy" citing an impact from weak U.S. dollar

** The brokerage says strong euro against U.S. dollar raises Airbus' costs, affecting its valuation despite the company's near-term hedging of currency exposure

** Citi cuts its PT for Airbus by 12% to 183 euros

** "We estimate 1 cent USD weakening reduces Airbus fair value by EUR 3-3.5 per share," it says

** The EUR/USD exchange rate is a principal driver of value for Airbus alongside long-term production rates of its A320 aircraft, the broker notes

** It expects Airbus' mid-term growth to be lower than its peers, and says lower valuation multiples are appropriate

** Out of 20 analysts that cover Airbus, 16 rate it "strong buy" or "buy", ​three "hold" and one "sell" - LSEG data

data.title: Exclusive: Aon crisis management director for credit and political risk Minor exits
data.description:
(The Insurer) - Aon's director of crisis management for structured credit and political risk John Minor has exited the broker, two senior sources told The Insurer.

The sources said that Minor had left to take up another position within the industry. The Insurer could not confirm this role prior to publication.

Chicago-based Minor has spent nearly a decade at Aon. He joined in November 2015 from his role as executive vice president and managing director at Alliant Emerging Markets.

Prior to this, he spent a further 11 years at Aon, latterly as director of political risk.

Aon declined to comment.

data.title: Sumber Alfaria Trijaya's Lawson Outlets Acquisition Appears Positive — Market Talk
data.description: Sumber Alfaria Trijaya's acquisition of Lawson outlets in Indonesia appears positive, UOB Kay Hian's Willinoy Sitorus says in a research report. The convenience store chain operator last month acquired a 70% stake in Lancar Wiguna Sejahtera, which manages Lawson outlets in Indonesia, from Midi Utama Indonesia. The deal strengthens Sumber Alfaria Trijaya's ready-to-eat product offerings, expands its customer reach and allows Lawson to leverage the company's robust logistics network. However, the brokerage lowers its 2025 and 2026 net-profit assumptions for the Indonesian company by 15.2% and 18.3%, respectively, due to higher operating expenses. It also cut the stock's target price to IDR3,000.00 from IDR3,400.00 with an unchanged buy rating. Shares last closed at IDR2,540.00. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

For further information:

Picton

Kathy Thompson, Company Secretary

020 7011 9988, kathy.thompson@picton.co.uk

About Picton

Established in 2005, Picton is listed on the main market of the London Stock Exchange and is a constituent of a number of EPRA indices including the FTSE EPRA Nareit Global Index.

Picton owns and actively manages a £723 million UK commercial property portfolio, invested across 47 assets and with around 350 occupiers (as at 31 March 2025).

Through an occupier focused, opportunity led approach, Picton aims to be the consistently best performing diversified UK REIT and has delivered upper quartile outperformance and a consistently higher income return than the MSCI UK Quarterly Property Index since launch. 

With a portfolio strategically positioned to capture income and capital growth, currently weighted towards the industrial sector, Picton's agile business model provides flexibility to adapt to evolving market trends over the long-term.

Picton has a responsible approach to business and is committed to being net zero carbon by 2040.

For more information please visit:www.picton.co.uk

data.title: Autobacs Seven's Total Store Sales Increase 9% in May
data.description:
Autobacs Seven booked a 9.1% increase in total store sales in May, with same-store sales up 7.7% year on year, according to a Friday filing with the Tokyo Stock Exchange.

The average purchase price per customer rose 8.8% across all stores.

The company attributed the sales growth to heightened demand for tires ahead of an upcoming price hike, while services and maintenance also remained strong, supported by a rise in vehicles undergoing safety inspections.

data.title: Marks Electrical CFO to Step Down
data.description:
By Maitane Sardon

Marks Electrical said its finance chief is leaving the company and that it has started to look for a successor.

The U.K. online electrical retailer said Friday that chief financial officer Josh Egan is stepping down to take up a new role as chief financial officer of Roadchef, a motorway service areas operator owned by Macquarie Group.

The company is set to publish its preliminary results for the 12 months ended March 31 on June 25.

Write to Maitane Sardon at maitane.sardon@wsj.com

data.title: European Central Bank Nearing End of Rate Cut Cycle, Berenberg Says
data.description:
Berenberg expects the European Central Bank's latest 25-basis-point rate cut to be the last in its current monetary policy easing cycle, citing "clear hints" from ECB President Christine Lagarde during a press conference on Thursday.

In a same-day report, the research firm noted Lagarde's repeated comments on the ECB being "well-positioned" to navigate the prevailing uncertain environment due to global trade tensions. Nevertheless, the ECB president reiterated the governing council's "data-dependent and meeting-by-meeting approach" in its future monetary policy decisions.

For Berenberg, it is not ruling out "some fine-tuning" from the ECB, but anticipates that any potential move from the central bank will depend on future developments, particularly the ongoing trade dispute between the European Union and the US.

"We do not expect the ECB to lower its deposit rate below 2% unless the trade war with the US escalates and the economy deteriorates as a result," the research firm wrote. "We anticipate that the two parties will reach a preliminary agreement by early July at the latest."

data.title: Danish Current Account Surplus Largest in 6 Months
data.description:
Denmark’s seasonally adjusted current account surplus widened to DKK 40.7 billion in April 2025, from DKK 33.7 billion in the previous month.

This marked the largest surplus since October last year, as the goods account surplus increased to DKK 33.4 billion from DKK 26.2 billion, due to a 7% rise in exports.

At the same time, the services account surplus expanded to DKK 4.4 billion from DKK 0.2 billion.

Conversely, the primary income surplus narrowed to DKK 5.8 billion from DKK 10.1 billion, while the secondary income deficit widened to DKK 2.9 billion from DKK 2.8 billion in March.

data.title: Financials, realty boost Indian shares after RBI's steep rate cut
data.description:
Indian shares advanced on Friday, reversing an early dip, after the Reserve Bank of India cut its key repo rate by a larger-than-expected 50 basis points in a bid to support economic growth.

The central bank also unexpectedly cut banks' Cash Reserve Ratio (CRR) by 100 basis points and changed its policy stance to neutral from accommodative.

The Nifty 50 rose 0.94% to 24,982.25, while the BSE Sensex traded 0.9% higher at 82,163.22 as of 11:17 a.m. IST. The indexes were down about 0.2% ahead of the RBI policy decision.

"It is a timely yet balanced intervention by the RBI that supports growth without compromising on macroeconomic stability," said Mahendra Patil, founder and managing partner at MP Financial Advisory Services.

Twelve of the 13 major sectors advanced. The broader, more domestically-focussed smallcaps and midcaps gained about 0.4% and 0.7%, respectively.

Rate sensitive sector realty gained 3.5% while auto added 1%, as the rate cut fuelled growth expectations.

High-weight financials erased early losses, rising 2% and hitting a record high.

The heaviest stock in Indian benchmark indexes, private lender HDFC Bank gained 2%.

RBI's decision to cut the CRR requirement for lenders will increase the banking system liquidity by 2.5 trillion rupees, ensure sufficient liquidity in the system in near future, said Abhishek Bisen, head of fixed income at Kotak Mahindra AMC.

Non-bank lender Bajaj Finance jumped 4.2% after RBI governor Sanjay Malhotra said stress in unsecured loans and credit cards has eased even if pressure continued to persist in the microfinance segment.

Ashok Leyland gained 3.4% after Morgan Stanley reiterated "overweight" and hiked its target price.

On the flipside, Bharti Hexacom fell 4% after Motilal Oswal downgraded the stock to "neutral" from "buy", citing unattractive risk-reward at current price.

data.title: Fiserv: Transaction Expected to Close in 3Q >FI
Stocks on guard for payrolls, Tesla tumbles as Trump-Musk bromance sours
data.description:
Asian shares slipped on Friday as investors hunkered down for the all-important U.S. payrolls report, while Tesla suffered huge losses on the very public feud between President Donald Trump and billionaire Elon Musk.

A run of soft economic data this week has markets wary of a downside surprise in the monthly payrolls print due later in the day, which would add to fears of stagflation while piling pressure on the Federal Reserve to ease policy in a hurry.

Tesla shares bounced 0.8% in after-hours trading after tumbling a whopping 14% overnight to wipe off $150 billion in market value. That came after Trump threatened to cut off government contracts to Elon Musk's companies as the once close relationship turned into a bitter open disagreement.

There were signs that tempers may be cooling a bit, with Trump telling Politico that "it's okay" when asked about the relationship and that White House aides had scheduled a call on Friday with Musk to broker a peace.

Nasdaq futures rose 0.3% and S&P 500 futures gained 0.4%, while the losses in European stock futures narrowed, with EUROSTOXX 50 futures down just 0.1%.

MSCI's broadest index of Asia-Pacific shares outside Japan (.MIAPJ0000PUS) slipped 0.2% on Friday away from its eight-month peak. It is still set for a weekly rise of 2.1%.

Chinese blue chips eased 0.2% and Hong Kong's Hang Seng dropped 0.5% as a call between Trump and Chinese President Xi Jinping offered little clarity to ease ongoing trade tensions.

"I think the fact that they are talking, the fact that there is a willingness to find a way through and the channels of communication are open is a positive," said Luke Yeaman, chief economist at the Commonwealth Bank of Australia.

"But I think it's clear that there are still a lot of tensions in the relationship and that neither side wants to give too much away… There's not a lot of goodwill to work with to fundamentally improve the trade relationship."

Most Asian shares are down slightly, but Japan's Nikkei is a rare bright spot in Asia, up 0.4%, helping trim its weekly drop to 0.7%.

WAIT FOR PAYROLLS

Weaker-than-expected labour market data, including a 47% year-on-year jump in Challenger layoffs and a significant downside surprise in ADP's private payrolls, have dampened expectations for the payrolls report.

Forecasts are centred on a rise of 130,000 jobs in May, with the unemployment rate holding steady at 4.2%.

Any unexpected weakness could bring the next U.S. rate cut forward and trigger a huge rally in Treasuries. Futures imply scant chances of a rate cut until September, which is about 93% priced in, with another move likely to come in December.

Yields on the benchmark ten-year Treasuries were flat at 4.3887%, having risen 3 basis points overnight to bounce away from a one-month low.

"We expect payrolls to lose additional momentum in May, printing a below-consensus 110,000," said analysts at TD Securities in a note to clients.

"Markets have recently been singularly focused on tariffs and deficits, with macro taking a back seat in recent weeks. Our forecast may not be sufficient to catalyze this revamped focus on macro, but we expect downside surprises to generate a larger market reaction."

The dollar was 0.2% higher against its major peers on Friday just a touch above a six-week low as soft economic data dent the U.S. currency.

The euro hit a six-week top of $1.1495 overnight after the European Central Bank cut rates but signaled that it was nearing the end of its year-long policy easing cycle. Investors have given up on a move in July, with the final move most likely to come in October or December.

In commodities markets, oil prices were slightly lower but were headed for weekly gains on supply concerns. U.S. crude futures slipped 0.4% to $63.12 a barrel but were up 3.8% for the week.

In precious metals, gold prices (=XAU) climbed 0.4% to $3,366.78 an ounce. For the week, they are up 2.3%.

data.title: 17LIVE Group Buys Back More Shares
data.description:
17LIVE Group repurchased 50,000 shares for SG$47,996 on Thursday, according to a same-day filing on the Singapore Exchange.

The social entertainment platform has been authorized to buy back around 18.2 million shares. To date, it has bought back 733,700 shares.

data.title: Italian-German 10-Year Yield Spread Nears Post-Eurozone-Debt Crisis Low — Market Talk
data.description: The 10-year Italian BTP-German Bund yield spread is nearing the post-eurozone-debt-crisis low of around 90 basis points, Commerzbank Research's rates strategists say. "The common driver for eurozone government bond convergence seems to be speculation about deeper EU integration and more joint [bond] issuance," the strategists say. Commerzbank Research maintains a positive stance ahead of the summer, with 10-year BTP-Bund yield spread above 90 basis points and 10-year French OAT-Bund yield spread above 60 basis points. On Thursday, the 10-year BTP-Bund spread closed at 95 basis points and the 10-year OAT-Bund spread finished at 67 basis points, according to Tradeweb. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

data.title: Halifax: UK Monthly Average House Prices Down 0.4% in May
data.description:
Britain's average house prices fell 0.4% month over month in May, following a 0.3% gain in April, according to data from Halifax published Friday.

Analysts expected a 0.4% growth for the month.

On a yearly basis, average house prices in the UK were 2.5% higher, compared with the 3.2% increase in the previous month.

data.title: German Posts Smallest Trade Surplus in 6 Months
data.description:
Germany's trade surplus dropped sharply to EUR 14.6 billion in April 2025, down from an upwardly revised EUR 21.3 billion in March and well below market forecasts of EUR 20.2 billion.

This marked the smallest surplus since October 2024, as exports declined and imports increased.

Exports fell by 1.7% month-over-month to a three-month low of EUR 131.1 billion—worse than the expected 0.5% decline—mainly due to lower sales to the US, China, and the U.K. Meanwhile, imports rose by 3.9% to a 23-month high of EUR 116.5 billion, rebounding from a 1.4% drop in March.

data.title: German Industrial Production Falls as Tariffs Pull Exports Lower
data.description:
By Ed Frankl

German industrial production declined in the first month of President Trump's global tariff blitz, with exports to the U.S. from Europe's largest economy also falling sharply.

Output contracted 1.4% on month, German statistics agency Destatis said Friday, a sharper fall than the 1.0% decline expected by economists polled by The Wall Street Journal.

That offsets much of the 2.3% jump in output in March, when U.S. firms stockpiled imports to get ahead of the impact from expected tariffs.

Production had been improving at the start of 2025, having posted the largest quarterly increase in the first three months of the year since the start of 2022, ahead of Russia's full-scale invasion of Ukraine.

Production dived 20% for the export-orientated pharmaceutical industry after a strong rise in March, with machinery production also feeding the overall decline, Destatis said.

Trade data, also published Friday, showed goods exports fell 1.7% on month in April, with exports to the U.S. sinking 10.5% as companies adapted to the new post-"Liberation Day" trade reality.

Write to Ed Frankl at edward.frankl@wsj.com

data.title: Easton Biopharmaceuticals Gets Registration Certificate for Axitinib Tablets; Shares Down 3%
data.description:
Chengdu Easton Biopharmaceuticals' axitinib tablets received a drug registration certificate from China's National Medical Products Administration, according to a Shanghai bourse filing on Friday.

The drug was originally developed by Pfizer to treat adult patients with advanced renal cell carcinoma, who have failed prior treatment with one tyrosine kinase inhibitor or cytokine therapy, the pharmaceutical company said.

The company's shares fell less than 3% in recent trade.

data.title: Moody's cuts Nissan corporate family rating to Ba2
data.description:
Global ratings agency Moody's on Friday cut Nissan Motor Co Ltd's corporate family rating to Ba2 from Ba1, as the embattled Japanese automaker seeks to push through a turnaround.

The outlook remains negative, Moody's said.

"The downgrade reflects the deterioration and expectation for continuing weakness in Nissan's credit profile, most notably in its automotive free cash flow and EBIT margin," Dean Enjo, Moody's ratings vice president and senior analyst, said in a statement.

Japan's third-biggest automaker last month unveiled sweeping new cost cuts, saying it would reduce its workforce by around 15% and cut production plants to 10 from 17 globally, as performance in its key markets continues to come under pressure.

data.title: German exports, industrial production fall more than expected in April
data.description:
German exports and industrial output fell more than expected in April as demand from the United States decreased following months of strong purchases in anticipation of U.S. tariffs.

German exports fell by 1.7% in April compared with the previous month, data from the federal statistics office showed on Friday.

The result compared with a 0.7% decrease forecast in a Reuters poll.

German industrial production fell by 1.4% in April compared to the previous month, the federal statistics office said on Friday.

Analysts polled by Reuters had predicted a 1.0% fall.

German industrial orders unexpectedly rose 0.6% on the month in April, driven by strong domestic demand, official data showed on Thursday.
